Were You Asked This?
Design Google Photos

Design a photo storage and sharing system that allows users to upload pictures, view them in an infinite scroll interface, and navigate through photos with swipe gestures in an enlarged view.

Asked at:

Google

Google Photos is a cloud photo storage and sharing platform where people back up images from phones and the web, then browse them in an infinite scroll, tap to see a full-screen view, and swipe through quickly. Think of the smooth, near-instant experience of scrolling a large library, with thumbnails that pop in fast and full-resolution images that appear as you swipe.

Interviewers ask this because it blends large-blob handling, high-throughput ingestion, background processing (thumbnails, metadata extraction), and low-latency read paths with stable pagination. It also tests your ability to separate hot paths (reads, scrolling, swiping) from cold paths (uploads, processing), apply CDN/object storage patterns, and reason about consistency, cost, and access control at scale.

Community Solutions
Design Google Photos
by siddhant ganeriwal
1
5
Design Google Photos Collections
by antriksh.don
0
3
Common Functional Requirements

Most candidates end up covering this set of core functionalities

Users should be able to upload photos reliably from mobile and web, including large files and flaky networks.

Users should be able to browse their library in an infinite scroll with fast loading and a stable, predictable order.

Users should be able to tap a photo to view it in an enlarged mode and swipe left/right seamlessly between adjacent photos.

Users should be able to organize and share photos or albums with configurable permissions and shareable links.

Common Deep Dives

Common follow-up questions interviewers like to ask for this question

How will you deliver low-latency, smooth infinite scroll with a consistent ordering for very large libraries?

How do you design the upload and processing pipeline for large photos so uploads are reliable and UI stays responsive?

How will you keep storage and bandwidth costs under control as the library scales to billions of photos?

How do you enforce sharing and privacy at scale without hurting cacheability or leaking data?

Relevant Patterns

Relevant patterns that you should know for this question

Handling Large Blobs

Photos are large binary objects; you need object storage, multipart/resumable uploads, presigned URLs, and CDN delivery. This pattern prevents app servers and databases from becoming the bottleneck when handling heavy media traffic.

Read More
Scaling Reads

Infinite scroll and swipe are read-heavy, latency-sensitive paths. You must design stable keyset pagination, caching, prefetching, and CDN strategies to keep p95/p99 low as libraries and traffic grow.

Read More
Multi-step Processes

Ingestion requires multiple steps—EXIF extraction, orientation fixes, deduplication, and multi-size thumbnail generation. A workflow or saga-like approach keeps the pipeline reliable, observable, and idempotent.

Read More
Relevant Technologies

Relevant technologies that could be used to solve this question

Cassandra

Cassandra excels at high-throughput, low-latency writes and time-ordered range scans when you model partitions by user and time. It’s a strong fit for photo metadata, pagination cursors, and album membership indexes.

Read More
Redis

Redis provides ultra-fast caching for recent page windows, ACL lookups, rate limits, and precomputed next/previous pointers used for swipe. It helps keep infinite scroll and full-screen navigation under tight latency budgets.

Read More
Kafka

Kafka decouples uploads from processing so thumbnail generation, EXIF parsing, and dedup can be handled asynchronously with backpressure, retries, and exactly-once/idempotent semantics at the consumer level.

Read More
Similar Problems to Practice

Related problems to practice for this question

Instagram

Both center on media ingestion, derivative generation, CDN delivery, and low-latency feed-like browsing with swipe gestures, plus privacy/sharing controls.

Read
Practice
Dropbox

Handling large binary uploads, resumable transfer, object storage, and background processing of file metadata are directly parallel to a photo service’s ingestion pipeline.

Read
Practice
FB News Feed

Infinite scroll, pagination stability, caching, and fast read paths under heavy load mirror the challenges of delivering a personalized, continuously loading feed.

Read
Practice
Red Flags to Avoid

Common mistakes that can sink candidates in an interview

Serving images directly from application servers or a relational database

This creates massive bandwidth and I/O contention on your app tier, drives up costs, and leads to poor latency. Photos should live in object storage and be delivered via CDN with presigned or public URLs.

Synchronous upload pipeline that blocks the user until thumbnails and metadata are processed

Coupling upload with processing increases tail latency and makes the UI feel slow or fragile on mobile networks. It also eliminates backpressure and retry mechanisms that a proper asynchronous workflow would provide.

Offset-based pagination for infinite scroll

Offset pagination produces duplicates/missed items under concurrent writes and becomes slow at high offsets. Use keyset/cursor pagination with a stable sort key to ensure consistent ordering and low latency.
